problems.problem_name: The Two-Children Probability Puzzle
problems.problem_text: Compute the following two probabilities:

a.problem_text:
A family has two children. Given that there is \emph{at least one boy}, what is the
conditional probability that \emph{both} children are boys?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: State the model (equally likely outcomes).}
Assume:
\begin{itemize}
\item Each child is equally likely to be a boy (B) or girl (G).
\item The two children's genders are independent.
\item Birth order matters (older, younger).
\end{itemize}
Then the sample space is
\[
\Omega=\{BB, BG, GB, GG\},
\]
and each outcome has probability $1/4$.

\par\textbf{Step 2: Define events.}
Let
\[
A=\{\text{both children are boys}\}=\{BB\},
\]
\[
B=\{\text{at least one boy}\}=\{BB,BG,GB\}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Compute the conditional probability.}
By definition,
\[
\mathbb{P}(A\mid B)=\frac{\mathbb{P}(A\cap B)}{\mathbb{P}(B)}.
\]
Here $A\cap B = A$ because $BB$ certainly implies at least one boy. Hence
\[
\mathbb{P}(A\mid B)=\frac{\mathbb{P}(BB)}{\mathbb{P}(BB\cup BG\cup GB)}
=\frac{1/4}{3/4}
=\frac{1}{3}.
\]

b.problem_text:
A family has two children. Given that \emph{one child is a boy}, what is the probability
that the other (second) child is also a boy?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: List the equally likely outcomes.}
With birth order (older, younger), the sample space is
\[
\Omega=\{BB,\ BG,\ GB,\ GG\},
\]
each with probability $1/4$.

\par\textbf{Step 2: A specific child is known to be a boy.}
Suppose the older child is known to be a boy. Then the possible outcomes are
\[
\{BB,\ BG\},
\]
each equally likely (both had prior probability $1/4$ and we condition on ``older is B'').
Thus
\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{both boys}\mid \text{older is boy})
=
\frac{\mathbb{P}(BB)}{\mathbb{P}(BB)+\mathbb{P}(BG)}
=
\frac{1/4}{1/4+1/4}
=
\frac{1}{2}.
\]
So
\[
\boxed{\mathbb{P}(\text{second is boy}\mid \text{a specific child is boy})=\frac{1}{2}.}
\]

problems.comment:
\textbf{Why the two cases give different answers.}

The difference comes from \emph{how the information is obtained}, which determines the
\emph{conditioning event}.

\par\textbf{Case 1: A specific child is known to be a boy.}
Here the information refers to a particular individual (e.g. ``the older child is a boy''
or ``the child you met is a boy'').  
This eliminates exactly half of the sample space:
\[
\{BB,\ BG\},
\]
which remain equally likely. Hence the probability that the other child is a boy is $1/2$.

\par\textbf{Case 2: You only know that at least one child is a boy.}
This information does not specify \emph{which} child is a boy.  
The possible families are
\[
\{BB,\ BG,\ GB\},
\]
which are equally likely under the standard model. Since only one of these three outcomes
has two boys, the probability becomes $1/3$.

\par\textbf{Key intuition.}
The two situations condition on \emph{different events}:
\[
\text{specific child is B} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{at least one B}.
\]
The second event is more likely to occur in mixed-gender families ($BG$ or $GB$) than in $BB$,
so conditioning on it \emph{over-represents mixed families}, reducing the probability of $BB$.

\par\textbf{General principle.}
In conditional probability, the result depends not only on the information itself,
but also on the \emph{mechanism by which the information is revealed}.
Different information mechanisms correspond to different conditioning events and can lead to
different posterior probabilities.
problems.keyword: conditional probability; Bayes conditioning; information mechanism; sampling bias; conditioning event; two-child problem; boy–girl paradox; specific child vs at least one; sample space reduction; equally likely outcomes; selection effect; posterior probability; probability puzzles; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Probability of Strictly Increasing Dice Rolls
problems.problem_text:
Roll a fair six-sided die three times (in order), obtaining $(X_1,X_2,X_3)$.
What is the probability that the results are strictly increasing:
\[
X_1 < X_2 < X_3 \ ?
\]
problems.hint: Conditioning on three values are distinct...

hints.hint_text: Conditioning on three values are distinct...

problems.keyword: dice rolls; strictly increasing sequence; ordered outcomes; combinatorics; combinations; permutations; sample space counting; discrete uniform distribution; distinct values; monotonic sequence; probability counting method; symmetry; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Probability of Consecutive Sixes in Three Dice Rolls
problems.problem_text:
Roll a fair six-sided die $3$ times. What is the probability of getting two sixes in a row
(i.e., at least one occurrence of the consecutive pattern $66$)?

hints.hint_text: List all the possibilities.

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Define the sample space.}
There are $6^3$ equally likely outcomes for three independent rolls.

\par\textbf{Step 2: Define events for each possible location of $66$.}
Let
\[
A=\{\text{rolls 1 and 2 are both }6\},\qquad
B=\{\text{rolls 2 and 3 are both }6\}.
\]
We want
\[
\mathbb{P}(A\cup B).
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Compute $\mathbb{P}(A)$ and $\mathbb{P}(B)$.}
By independence,
\[
\mathbb{P}(A)=\mathbb{P}(X_1=6, X_2=6)=\frac{1}{6}\cdot\frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{36},
\]
\[
\mathbb{P}(B)=\mathbb{P}(X_2=6, X_3=6)=\frac{1}{36}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Compute the intersection $\mathbb{P}(A\cap B)$.}
Both $A$ and $B$ occur iff all three rolls are $6$:
\[
A\cap B=\{X_1=6,X_2=6,X_3=6\}.
\]
Thus
\[
\mathbb{P}(A\cap B)=\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^3=\frac{1}{216}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 5: Use inclusion-exclusion.}
\[
\mathbb{P}(A\cup B)=\mathbb{P}(A)+\mathbb{P}(B)-\mathbb{P}(A\cap B)
=\frac{1}{36}+\frac{1}{36}-\frac{1}{216}.
\]
Compute:
\[
\frac{1}{36}+\frac{1}{36}=\frac{2}{36}=\frac{1}{18}=\frac{12}{216},
\]
so
\[
\mathbb{P}(A\cup B)=\frac{12}{216}-\frac{1}{216}=\frac{11}{216}.
\]

problems.keyword: dice rolls; consecutive outcomes; runs; pattern probability; inclusion–exclusion; overlapping events; independent trials; discrete probability; sample space counting; binomial probability; conditional independence; combinatorics; probability of runs; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Divisibility of the Sum of Ten Dice by 6
problems.problem_text:
Roll $10$ fair six-sided dice simultaneously. What is the probability that the sum
\[
S:=X_1+\cdots+X_{10}
\]
is divisible by $6$?
problems.hint: Conditioning on the first 9 tosses, i.e., ($X_1+\cdots+X_{9}) \bmod 6$.

hints.hint_text: Conditioning on the first 9 tosses, i.e., ($X_1+\cdots+X_{9}) \bmod 6$.

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Work modulo $6$.}
Let $X_i\in\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ be i.i.d. uniform. Consider residues modulo $6$:
\[
X_i \bmod 6 \in \{0,1,2,3,4,5\}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Show one die is uniform modulo $6$.}
Each face occurs with probability $1/6$, and the residues mod $6$ appear exactly once:
\[
1\equiv 1,\ 2\equiv 2,\ 3\equiv 3,\ 4\equiv 4,\ 5\equiv 5,\ 6\equiv 0 \pmod 6.
\]
Hence for every $r\in\{0,1,2,3,4,5\}$,
\[
\mathbb{P}(X_i \equiv r \!\!\!\pmod 6)=\frac{1}{6}.
\]
So $X_i \bmod 6$ is uniform on $\mathbb{Z}_6$.

\par\textbf{Step 3: Sum of independent uniform residues is uniform.}
Let $U,V$ be independent uniform random variables on $\mathbb{Z}_6$.
Then for any residue $a\in\mathbb{Z}_6$,
\[
\mathbb{P}(U+V\equiv a \!\!\!\pmod 6)
=
\sum_{r\in\mathbb{Z}_6}\mathbb{P}(U\equiv r)\,\mathbb{P}(V\equiv a-r)
=
\sum_{r\in\mathbb{Z}_6}\frac{1}{6}\cdot\frac{1}{6}
=
6\cdot\frac{1}{36}
=
\frac{1}{6}.
\]
Thus $U+V \bmod 6$ is also uniform. By induction, the sum of any number of i.i.d. uniform
$\mathbb{Z}_6$ variables remains uniform on $\mathbb{Z}_6$.

\par Applying this to $X_1,\dots,X_{10}$, we get that
\[
S \bmod 6 \ \text{is uniform on }\mathbb{Z}_6.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Read off the desired probability.}
We want $6\mid S$, i.e. $S\equiv 0\pmod 6$. Since $S\bmod 6$ is uniform,
\[
\mathbb{P}(6\mid S)=\mathbb{P}(S\equiv 0\!\!\!\pmod 6)=\frac{1}{6}.
\]

problems.keyword: modular arithmetic; modulo 6; dice sum; uniform distribution; residue classes; cyclic group; independence; convolution; sum of random variables; uniform modulo property; probability of divisibility; random walk on integers mod n; symmetry argument; discrete probability; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Recurrence for the Probability of an Even Number of Heads
problems.problem_text:
A biased coin with $\mathbb{P}(H)=p$ is tossed $n$ times. Let $E_n$ be the event that the
total number of heads after $n$ tosses is even. Find a recurrence relation for
\[
a_n := \mathbb{P}(E_n).
\]

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Condition on the last toss.}
After $n$ tosses, the number of heads is even iff:
\begin{itemize}
\item the first $n-1$ tosses have an even number of heads and the $n$th toss is $T$, or
\item the first $n-1$ tosses have an odd number of heads and the $n$th toss is $H$.
\end{itemize}

Let $a_{n-1}=\mathbb{P}(\text{even $\#$ heads after }n-1\text{ tosses})$.
Then $\mathbb{P}(\text{odd after }n-1)=1-a_{n-1}$.

\par Therefore,
\[
a_n = \mathbb{P}(E_{n-1})\mathbb{P}(T) + \mathbb{P}(E_{n-1}^c)\mathbb{P}(H)
= a_{n-1}(1-p) + (1-a_{n-1})p.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Simplify the recurrence.}
\[
a_n = a_{n-1}(1-p) + p - pa_{n-1}
= p + (1-2p)a_{n-1}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Give an initial condition.}
For $n=0$ tosses, the number of heads is $0$ (even), so
\[
a_0 = 1.
\]
Equivalently, for $n=1$:
\[
a_1 = \mathbb{P}(\text{0 heads}) = 1-p.
\]

problems.keyword: biased coin; Bernoulli trials; recurrence relation; even number of heads; parity; conditional probability; dynamic programming; Markov recursion; probability transition; binomial process; state transition; expectation recursion; stochastic process; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: First Occurrence of $HH$ vs. $TH$ in Coin Tossing
problems.problem_text:
Flip a fair coin repeatedly until either the pattern $HH$ or the pattern $TH$ appears
as two consecutive flips. If $HH$ appears first, you win; if $TH$ appears first, your friend wins.
Find $\mathbb{P}(\text{you win})$.
problems.hint: If $T$ shows up, then $TH$ will always show up before $HH$.

hints.hint_text: If $T$ shows up, then $TH$ will always show up before $HH$.

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Identify the state information needed.}
The stopping patterns have length $2$, so the only relevant information is the \emph{most recent flip}.
Define states:
\[
S=\emptyset \ (\text{no previous flip yet}),\qquad
S=H \ (\text{last flip was }H),\qquad
S=T \ (\text{last flip was }T).
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Let $p_H$ and $p_T$ be the win probabilities from each state.}
Let
\[
p_H := \mathbb{P}(\text{you eventually win}\mid \text{current state }H),
\qquad
p_T := \mathbb{P}(\text{you eventually win}\mid \text{current state }T).
\]
From the start (no previous flip), by symmetry of the first toss,
\[
p := \mathbb{P}(\text{you win}) = \tfrac12 p_H + \tfrac12 p_T.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Write recursion for $p_H$.}
If the current state is $H$, the next flip is:
\begin{itemize}
\item $H$ with probability $\tfrac12$, yielding $HH$ immediately $\Rightarrow$ you win.
\item $T$ with probability $\tfrac12$, yielding last flip $T$ (no stop yet) $\Rightarrow$ state becomes $T$.
\end{itemize}
Therefore
\[
p_H = \frac12\cdot 1 + \frac12\cdot p_T.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Write recursion for $p_T$.}
If the current state is $T$, the next flip is:
\begin{itemize}
\item $H$ with probability $\tfrac12$, yielding $TH$ immediately $\Rightarrow$ friend wins, so your win prob is $0$.
\item $T$ with probability $\tfrac12$, yielding last flip $T$ again (still no stop) $\Rightarrow$ state remains $T$.
\end{itemize}
Thus
\[
p_T = \frac12\cdot 0 + \frac12\cdot p_T.
\]
Solve:
\[
p_T = \frac12 p_T \quad \Longrightarrow \quad p_T = 0.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 5: Solve for $p_H$ and then $p$.}
Plug $p_T=0$ into the equation for $p_H$:
\[
p_H = \frac12\cdot 1 + \frac12\cdot 0 = \frac12.
\]
Then the overall win probability from the start is
\[
p = \frac12 p_H + \frac12 p_T = \frac12\cdot \frac12 + \frac12\cdot 0 = \frac14.
\]

problems.keyword: coin tossing; pattern matching; stopping time; Markov chain; recursive probability; first-step analysis; absorbing states; conditional probability; geometric waiting process; fair coin; sequential probability; competing patterns; state transition; stochastic process; hitting probability

problems.problem_name: Which Pattern Appears First in Coin Tossing?
problems.problem_text: Flip a fair coin repeatedly.

a.problem_text:
What is the probability that the pattern $HHT$ appears
before the pattern $HTT$?

hints.hint_text: Once HH appears, HHT will always appear first.

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Use states given by the longest relevant suffix.}
Since both target patterns have length $3$, we track the longest suffix of the past flips
that is also a prefix of either $HHT$ or $HTT$.

The relevant prefixes are:
\[
\emptyset,\quad H,\quad HH,\quad HT.
\]
These form the transient states. The absorbing states are:
\[
\text{Win: }HHT\ \text{ occurs},\qquad
\text{Lose: }HTT\ \text{ occurs}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Define win probabilities from each state.}
Let $p_\emptyset, p_H, p_{HH}, p_{HT}$ be the probabilities that $HHT$ occurs before $HTT$
given the current suffix state.
We want $p_\emptyset$.

\par\textbf{Step 3: Write first-step recursions.}

\par From state $\emptyset$ (no useful suffix yet):
\[
p_\emptyset = \tfrac12 p_H + \tfrac12 p_\emptyset,
\]
because an $H$ takes you to state $H$, while a $T$ keeps you at $\emptyset$.

\par From state $H$:
\[
p_H = \tfrac12 p_{HH} + \tfrac12 p_{HT},
\]
because next flip $H$ gives suffix $HH$, and next flip $T$ gives suffix $HT$.

\par From state $HH$:
\[
p_{HH} = \tfrac12 p_{HH} + \tfrac12 \cdot 1,
\]
because next flip $H$ keeps the last two flips as $HH$ (suffix remains $HH$),
while next flip $T$ completes $HHT$ immediately (win).

\par From state $HT$:
\[
p_{HT} = \tfrac12 p_H + \tfrac12 \cdot 0,
\]
because next flip $H$ makes the last flip $H$ (suffix $H$),
and next flip $T$ completes $HTT$ immediately (lose).

\par\textbf{Step 4: Solve the system.}

\par First solve for $p_{HH}$:
\[
p_{HH} = \tfrac12 p_{HH} + \tfrac12
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\tfrac12 p_{HH} = \tfrac12
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
p_{HH}=1.
\]

\par Next solve for $p_{HT}$ using $p_{HT}=\tfrac12 p_H$:
\[
p_{HT}=\tfrac12 p_H.
\]

\par Now use $p_H = \tfrac12 p_{HH} + \tfrac12 p_{HT}$:
\[
p_H = \tfrac12\cdot 1 + \tfrac12\left(\tfrac12 p_H\right)
= \tfrac12 + \tfrac14 p_H.
\]
So
\[
p_H - \tfrac14 p_H = \tfrac12
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\tfrac34 p_H = \tfrac12
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
p_H = \frac{2}{3}.
\]
Then
\[
p_{HT}=\tfrac12 p_H = \frac{1}{3}.
\]

\par Finally solve for $p_\emptyset$ from
\[
p_\emptyset = \tfrac12 p_H + \tfrac12 p_\emptyset
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\tfrac12 p_\emptyset = \tfrac12 p_H
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
p_\emptyset = p_H = \frac{2}{3}.
\]

b.problem_text:
What is the probability that the pattern $HHT$ appears
before the pattern $HTH$?

hints.hint_text: Once HH appears, HHT will always appear first.

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Choose states as longest relevant suffixes.}
Both patterns have length $3$, so we track the longest suffix of the current flip history
that is also a prefix of either $HHT$ or $HTH$.

The relevant prefix/suffix states are:
\[
\emptyset,\quad H,\quad HH,\quad HT.
\]
Absorbing outcomes:
\[
\text{Win if }HHT\text{ occurs first},\qquad \text{Lose if }HTH\text{ occurs first}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Define win probabilities from each state.}
Let
\[
p_\emptyset,\ p_H,\ p_{HH},\ p_{HT}
\]
denote the probability that $HHT$ occurs before $HTH$ given the current state.
We seek $p_\emptyset$.

\par\textbf{Step 3: Write first-step recursions (correct transitions).}

\par From state $\emptyset$:
\[
p_\emptyset=\tfrac12 p_H+\tfrac12 p_\emptyset,
\]
because a $T$ keeps you in $\emptyset$ and an $H$ moves you to state $H$.

\par From state $H$:
\[
p_H=\tfrac12 p_{HH}+\tfrac12 p_{HT},
\]
because after $H$:
\begin{itemize}
\item next $H$ gives suffix $HH$,
\item next $T$ gives suffix $HT$ (which is a prefix of $HTH$).
\end{itemize}

\par From state $HH$:
\[
p_{HH}=\tfrac12 p_{HH}+\tfrac12\cdot 1,
\]
because:
\begin{itemize}
\item next $H$ keeps suffix $HH$,
\item next $T$ completes $HHT$ immediately (win).
\end{itemize}

\par From state $HT$:
\[
p_{HT}=\tfrac12\cdot 0+\tfrac12 p_\emptyset,
\]
because:
\begin{itemize}
\item next $H$ completes $HTH$ immediately (lose),
\item next $T$ yields $\cdots HTT$, whose longest suffix that is a prefix of either target
is $\emptyset$ (both targets start with $H$).
\end{itemize}

\par\textbf{Step 4: Solve the system.}

\par First solve $p_{HH}$:
\[
p_{HH}=\tfrac12 p_{HH}+\tfrac12
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
p_{HH}=1.
\]

\par Next, from $p_\emptyset=\tfrac12 p_H+\tfrac12 p_\emptyset$ we get
\[
p_\emptyset=p_H.
\]

\par Use $p_{HT}=\tfrac12 p_\emptyset$ and plug into $p_H$:
\[
p_H=\tfrac12 p_{HH}+\tfrac12 p_{HT}
=\tfrac12\cdot 1+\tfrac12\left(\tfrac12 p_\emptyset\right)
=\tfrac12+\tfrac14 p_\emptyset.
\]
Since $p_H=p_\emptyset$, we obtain
\[
p_\emptyset=\tfrac12+\tfrac14 p_\emptyset
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\tfrac34 p_\emptyset=\tfrac12
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
p_\emptyset=\frac{2}{3}.
\]

c.problem_text:
What is the probability that the pattern $HHH$ appears
\emph{before} the pattern $THH$?

hints.hint_text: Once you ever see a $T$, you either immediately lose upon seeing the next two $H$'s in a row, or you keep seeing more $T$'s; in fact from state $T$ you never have a positive chance to win, so you must win \emph{before the first $T$ occurs}, i.e., by starting with $HHH$.

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Build states using the longest relevant suffix.}
Both target patterns have length $3$, so we track the longest suffix of the observed flips
that is also a prefix of either target pattern ($HHH$ or $THH$).

Relevant prefixes are:
\[
\emptyset,\quad H,\quad HH,\quad T,\quad TH.
\]
Absorbing states:
\[
\text{Win: }HHH,\qquad \text{Lose: }THH.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Write the transition rules.}
From each state, append the next flip (H or T) and keep the longest suffix that is a prefix above.

\begin{itemize}
\item From $\emptyset$: 
\[
H\to H,\qquad T\to T.
\]
\item From $H$:
\[
H\to HH,\qquad T\to T.
\]
\item From $HH$:
\[
H\to HHH\ (\text{win}),\qquad T\to T \quad (\text{since }HHT\text{ ends in }T).
\]
\item From $T$:
\[
H\to TH,\qquad T\to T.
\]
\item From $TH$:
\[
H\to THH\ (\text{lose}),\qquad T\to T \quad (\text{since }THT\text{ ends in }T).
\]
\end{itemize}

\par\textbf{Step 3: Define win probabilities from each state.}
Let
\[
p_\emptyset,\ p_H,\ p_{HH},\ p_T,\ p_{TH}
\]
denote the probability that $HHH$ occurs before $THH$ given the current state.
We want $p_\emptyset$.

Boundary conditions:
\[
p_{HHH}=1,\qquad p_{THH}=0.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: First-step recursions.}
Using the transitions above (fair coin $\Rightarrow$ each step has prob.\ $1/2$):

\[
p_\emptyset=\tfrac12 p_H+\tfrac12 p_T,
\]
\[
p_H=\tfrac12 p_{HH}+\tfrac12 p_T,
\]
\[
p_{HH}=\tfrac12\cdot 1+\tfrac12 p_T,
\]
\[
p_T=\tfrac12 p_{TH}+\tfrac12 p_T,
\]
\[
p_{TH}=\tfrac12\cdot 0+\tfrac12 p_T.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 5: Solve the system.}
From the $p_T$ equation:
\[
p_T=\tfrac12 p_{TH}+\tfrac12 p_T
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\tfrac12 p_T=\tfrac12 p_{TH}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
p_T=p_{TH}.
\]
But also $p_{TH}=\tfrac12 p_T$, hence
\[
p_T=\tfrac12 p_T \quad\Longrightarrow\quad p_T=0.
\]

Then
\[
p_{HH}=\tfrac12\cdot 1+\tfrac12\cdot 0=\tfrac12,
\]
\[
p_H=\tfrac12 p_{HH}+\tfrac12 p_T=\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac12+0=\tfrac14,
\]
\[
p_\emptyset=\tfrac12 p_H+\tfrac12 p_T=\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac14+0=\tfrac18.
\]

d.problem_text:
What is the probability that the pattern $HHT$ appears
\emph{before} the pattern $THH$?

hints.hint_text: If you ever see a $T$ before winning, you are in the ``$T$-world'' where $THH$ is a looming threat; in fact starting from $T$ you cannot win (both patterns start with $T$ or $HH$), so you must win by getting $HH$ before the first $T$.

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Choose states via the longest relevant suffix.}
Both target patterns have length $3$, so we track the longest suffix of the observed sequence
that is also a prefix of either $HHT$ or $THH$.

The relevant prefixes are:
\[
\emptyset,\quad H,\quad HH,\quad T,\quad TH.
\]
Absorbing states:
\[
\text{Win: }HHT,\qquad \text{Lose: }THH.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Write transitions between states.}
Append the next flip (H or T) and keep the longest suffix that is a prefix of one of the targets.

\begin{itemize}
\item From $\emptyset$:
\[
H\to H,\qquad T\to T.
\]

\item From $H$:
\[
H\to HH,\qquad T\to T \quad (\text{since }HT\text{ ends in }T).
\]

\item From $HH$:
\[
T\to HHT\ (\text{win}),
\qquad
H\to HH \quad (\text{since }HHH\text{ has suffix }HH).
\]

\item From $T$:
\[
H\to TH,\qquad T\to T.
\]

\item From $TH$:
\[
H\to THH\ (\text{lose}),
\qquad
T\to T \quad (\text{since }THT\text{ ends in }T).
\]
\end{itemize}

\par\textbf{Step 3: Define win probabilities from each state.}
Let
\[
p_\emptyset,\ p_H,\ p_{HH},\ p_T,\ p_{TH}
\]
be the probability that $HHT$ occurs before $THH$ given the current state.
We want $p_\emptyset$.

Boundary conditions:
\[
p_{HHT}=1,\qquad p_{THH}=0.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Set up first-step recursions (fair coin).}
\[
p_\emptyset = \tfrac12 p_H + \tfrac12 p_T,
\]
\[
p_H = \tfrac12 p_{HH} + \tfrac12 p_T,
\]
\[
p_{HH} = \tfrac12\cdot 1 + \tfrac12 p_{HH},
\]
\[
p_T = \tfrac12 p_{TH} + \tfrac12 p_T,
\]
\[
p_{TH} = \tfrac12\cdot 0 + \tfrac12 p_T.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 5: Solve for the $T$-side states.}
From
\[
p_T = \tfrac12 p_{TH} + \tfrac12 p_T
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
p_T = p_{TH}.
\]
But $p_{TH}=\tfrac12 p_T$, hence
\[
p_T = \tfrac12 p_T \quad\Longrightarrow\quad p_T=0,
\qquad\text{and thus }p_{TH}=0.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 6: Solve for the $H$-side states.}
From
\[
p_{HH} = \tfrac12\cdot 1 + \tfrac12 p_{HH}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\tfrac12 p_{HH}=\tfrac12
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
p_{HH}=1.
\]
Then
\[
p_H = \tfrac12 p_{HH} + \tfrac12 p_T
= \tfrac12\cdot 1 + 0
= \tfrac12.
\]
Finally
\[
p_\emptyset = \tfrac12 p_H + \tfrac12 p_T
= \tfrac12\cdot \tfrac12 + 0
= \tfrac14.
\]

problems.keyword: pattern matching; coin tossing; waiting time; stopping time; competing patterns; first occurrence probability; Markov chain; state space; prefix–suffix states; first-step analysis; recursive equations; absorbing states; hitting probability; stochastic process; fair coin; sequence overlap; Penney’s game; probability interview problem

problems.problem_name: Probability of Winning a Two-Point Lead Series
problems.problem_text:
Alice and Bob play independent rounds. Alice wins each round with probability $p$
(and Bob wins with probability $q:=1-p$). They keep playing until one player is ahead
by $2$ wins. What is the probability that Bob wins the overall series?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Model the score difference as a random walk.}
Let $D_n$ be the score difference after $n$ rounds:
\[
D_n := (\#\text{Alice wins})-(\#\text{Bob wins}).
\]
Initially $D_0=0$. Each round changes $D$ by
\[
D_{n+1}=
\begin{cases}
D_n+1, & \text{with probability }p \quad(\text{Alice wins}),\\
D_n-1, & \text{with probability }q \quad(\text{Bob wins}).
\end{cases}
\]
The game stops when $D_n=2$ (Alice is ahead by 2) or $D_n=-2$ (Bob is ahead by 2).

So this is a biased random walk on the states $\{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$ with absorbing boundaries
at $\pm 2$.

\par\textbf{Step 2: Define the hitting probability we want.}
Let
\[
u_k := \mathbb{P}(\text{Bob eventually wins} \mid D_0=k),
\qquad k\in\{-2,-1,0,1,2\}.
\]
We want $u_0$.

Boundary conditions:
\[
u_{-2}=1 \quad(\text{Bob already ahead by 2}),
\qquad
u_{2}=0 \quad(\text{Alice already ahead by 2}).
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Write the recursion (first-step analysis).}
For the interior states $k\in\{-1,0,1\}$, after one round:
\[
u_k = p\,u_{k+1} + q\,u_{k-1}.
\]
So we have the system:
\[
u_{-1}=p\,u_0+q\,u_{-2},\qquad
u_0=p\,u_1+q\,u_{-1},\qquad
u_1=p\,u_2+q\,u_0.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Solve the system.}
Use $u_{-2}=1$ and $u_2=0$.

\par From the equation for $u_1$:
\[
u_1 = p\cdot 0 + q\,u_0 = q\,u_0.
\]

\par From the equation for $u_{-1}$:
\[
u_{-1} = p\,u_0 + q\cdot 1 = p\,u_0 + q.
\]

\par Plug into the equation for $u_0$:
\[
u_0 = p\,u_1 + q\,u_{-1}
= p(q\,u_0) + q(p\,u_0+q)
= pq\,u_0 + pq\,u_0 + q^2
= 2pq\,u_0 + q^2.
\]
Thus
\[
u_0(1-2pq)=q^2.
\]
Since $1-2pq = p^2+q^2$ (because $(p+q)^2=1=p^2+2pq+q^2$), we get
\[
u_0 = \frac{q^2}{p^2+q^2}.
\]

problems.problem_name: Expected Waiting Time for Consecutive Heads
problems.problem_text: Toss a fair coin repeatedly.

a.problem_text:
What is the expected number of tosses until the pattern $HH$
(two heads in a row) first appears?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Use states = current run length of consecutive heads.}
Track the number of consecutive heads at the end of the current sequence. The states are
\[
0,\ 1,\ 2,
\]
where state $2$ is absorbing (we have seen $HH$).

\par\textbf{Step 2: Define expected remaining tosses from each state.}
Let
\[
E_k := \mathbb{E}[\text{additional tosses needed to reach state }2 \mid \text{currently in state }k],
\qquad k=0,1,2.
\]
We want $E_0$, and clearly
\[
E_2=0.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Write first-step recursions.}
From state $0$:
\[
E_0 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_1 + \tfrac12 E_0,
\]
because after one toss, with probability $1/2$ we flip $H$ and go to state $1$,
and with probability $1/2$ we flip $T$ and remain in state $0$.

\par From state $1$:
\[
E_1 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_2 + \tfrac12 E_0
= 1 + \tfrac12\cdot 0 + \tfrac12 E_0
= 1 + \tfrac12 E_0,
\]
because after one toss, with probability $1/2$ we flip $H$ and reach $HH$ (done),
and with probability $1/2$ we flip $T$ and reset to state $0$.

\par\textbf{Step 4: Solve the system.}
From the $E_0$ equation:
\[
E_0 - \tfrac12 E_0 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_0 = 2 + E_1.
\]
Substitute $E_1 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_0$:
\[
E_0 = 2 + 1 + \tfrac12 E_0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_0 - \tfrac12 E_0 = 3
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\tfrac12 E_0 = 3
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_0 = 6.
\]

b.problem_text:
What is the expected number of tosses until the pattern $HHH$
(three heads in a row) first appears?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Use states = current run length of consecutive heads.}
Let the state be the number of consecutive heads seen at the \emph{end} of the current sequence:
\[
0,\ 1,\ 2,\ 3,
\]
where state $3$ is absorbing (we have seen $HHH$).

\par\textbf{Step 2: Define expected remaining tosses from each state.}
Let
\[
E_k := \mathbb{E}[\text{additional tosses needed to reach state }3 \mid \text{currently in state }k],
\qquad k=0,1,2,3.
\]
We want $E_0$. Also
\[
E_3=0.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Write first-step recursions.}
From state $k\in\{0,1,2\}$, we take one toss (hence the leading $1$),
then:
\begin{itemize}
\item with probability $1/2$ we flip $H$ and move to state $k+1$,
\item with probability $1/2$ we flip $T$ and reset to state $0$.
\end{itemize}
Thus:
\[
E_0 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_1 + \tfrac12 E_0,
\]
\[
E_1 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_2 + \tfrac12 E_0,
\]
\[
E_2 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_3 + \tfrac12 E_0
= 1 + \tfrac12 E_0.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Solve the system.}
From the first equation:
\[
E_0 - \tfrac12 E_0 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_0 = 2 + E_1.
\]

From the third equation:
\[
E_2 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_0.
\]

Plug this into the second equation:
\[
E_1 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_2 + \tfrac12 E_0
= 1 + \tfrac12\left(1 + \tfrac12 E_0\right) + \tfrac12 E_0
= 1 + \tfrac12 + \tfrac14 E_0 + \tfrac12 E_0
= \tfrac32 + \tfrac34 E_0.
\]

Now substitute $E_0 = 2 + E_1$ into the last expression:
\[
E_1 = \tfrac32 + \tfrac34(2+E_1)
= \tfrac32 + \tfrac32 + \tfrac34 E_1
= 3 + \tfrac34 E_1.
\]
So
\[
E_1 - \tfrac34 E_1 = 3
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\tfrac14 E_1 = 3
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_1 = 12.
\]
Therefore
\[
E_0 = 2 + E_1 = 2 + 12 = 14.
\]

c.problem_text:
Let $T_n$ be the (random) number of tosses until the first time
$n$ consecutive heads ($H^n$) appear. Compute $\mathbb{E}[T_n].$

hints.hint_text: Derive the recursive relation between $\mathbb{E}[T_n]$ and $\mathbb{E}[T_{n-1}]$.

solution.text:
We derive a simple recursion for $m_n:=\mathbb{E}[T_n]$ and solve it.

\par\textbf{Step 1: Define the key state and use a Markov/renewal viewpoint.}
Consider the moment when you \emph{first} obtain $(n-1)$ consecutive heads.
From that moment on, the process has a ``memoryless'' restart property: what happens next depends
only on whether the next toss is $H$ or $T$.

\par\textbf{Step 2: Decompose $T_n$ into two parts.}
Write
\[
T_n = T_{n-1} + R_n,
\]
where $R_n$ is the \emph{additional} number of tosses needed to go from having $(n-1)$ consecutive heads
(at the first time you reach that state) to achieving $n$ consecutive heads.

Taking expectations gives
\[
m_n = m_{n-1} + \mathbb{E}[R_n].
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Compute $\mathbb{E}[R_n]$ by conditioning on the next toss.}
Starting from $(n-1)$ consecutive heads, toss once:
\begin{itemize}
\item With probability $1/2$, you get $H$ and finish immediately. Then $R_n=1$.
\item With probability $1/2$, you get $T$. Then you have spent $1$ toss and you are effectively back to
``starting over'' for $n$ consecutive heads, which takes an expected additional $m_n$ tosses.
So in this case $R_n = 1 + T_n'$ where $T_n'$ is an independent copy of $T_n$, hence
$\mathbb{E}[R_n \mid T]=1+m_n$.
\end{itemize}

Therefore
\[
\mathbb{E}[R_n]
= \frac12\cdot 1 + \frac12\cdot(1+m_n)
= \frac12 + \frac12 + \frac12 m_n
= 1 + \frac12 m_n.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Plug into the decomposition and solve for $m_n$.}
From $m_n = m_{n-1} + \mathbb{E}[R_n]$ we get
\[
m_n = m_{n-1} + 1 + \frac12 m_n.
\]
Rearrange:
\[
\frac12 m_n = m_{n-1} + 1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
m_n = 2m_{n-1} + 2.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 5: Add the base case and solve the recursion.}
For $n=1$, $T_1$ is geometric with success probability $1/2$, so
\[
m_1=\mathbb{E}[T_1]=2.
\]

Now solve
\[
m_n = 2m_{n-1}+2,\qquad m_1=2.
\]
Define $u_n := m_n+2$. Then
\[
u_n = (m_n+2)=2m_{n-1}+2+2=2(m_{n-1}+2)=2u_{n-1},
\]
with $u_1=m_1+2=4$. Hence
\[
u_n = 4\cdot 2^{n-1}=2^{n+1}.
\]
Therefore
\[
m_n = u_n - 2 = 2^{n+1}-2.
\]

problems.keyword: waiting time; consecutive heads; runs in Bernoulli trials; expected stopping time; geometric waiting; Markov chain; renewal argument; first-step analysis; recursive expectation; recurrence relation; state reset; fair coin; pattern occurrence; stochastic process; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Probability that Two Random Chords Intersect
problems.problem_text: You draw a circle and choose two chords at random. What is the probability that the two chords intersect?

solution.text:
\textbf{Important modeling note.}
“Random chord” is ambiguous (there are multiple non-equivalent ways to choose a chord uniformly).
A standard interview model is:

\par\emph{Model:} pick 4 points independently and uniformly on the circumference (with probability 1 they are distinct),
and form chord 1 from the first two points and chord 2 from the last two points.

Under this model, we compute $\mathbb{P}(\text{two chords intersect})$.

\par\textbf{Step 1: Reduce intersection to an ordering condition.}
Let the four sampled points be labeled by which chord-end they are:
\[
1,2 \text{ are endpoints of chord }C_1,\qquad 3,4 \text{ are endpoints of chord }C_2.
\]
Place these four labels in their circular order around the circumference.

\par\textbf{Key fact.}
Two chords intersect (in the interior) if and only if their endpoints \emph{alternate} around the circle.
Equivalently, moving around the circle, you see one endpoint of $C_1$, then one endpoint of $C_2$,
then the other endpoint of $C_1$, then the other endpoint of $C_2$.

So, in circular order, intersection occurs exactly when the labels appear as one of:
\[
1,3,2,4 \quad\text{or}\quad 1,4,2,3
\]
(up to rotation).

\par\textbf{Step 2: Count the possible circular orders.}
Fix label $1$ at some starting point (rotational symmetry). Then the remaining labels
$\{2,3,4\}$ can appear in any order as we go clockwise.

Hence there are
\[
3! = 6
\]
equally likely circular orders for $(2,3,4)$ relative to $1$.

\par\textbf{Step 3: Count how many of these orders produce an intersection.}
Chord $C_1$ has endpoints $1$ and $2$. For $C_2$ to cross $C_1$, the endpoints $3$ and $4$
must lie on opposite arcs determined by $1$ and $2$, i.e. they must alternate with $\{1,2\}$.

Given $1$ fixed, this happens exactly when $2$ is separated from $1$ by exactly one of $\{3,4\}$:
\[
(2,3,4) = (3,2,4)\quad \text{or}\quad (4,2,3).
\]
These are $2$ favorable orders out of $6$ total.

\par\textbf{Step 4: Compute the probability.}
Therefore,
\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{chords intersect})
=
\frac{2}{6}
=
\frac{1}{3}.
\]

problems.keyword: random chords; circle geometry; chord intersection; geometric probability; random points on circumference; circular order; alternating endpoints; combinatorial geometry; symmetry argument; rotational invariance; uniform distribution on circle; probability counting; Bertrand-type ambiguity; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Expected Waiting Time for Consecutive Outcomes in Dice Rolls
problems.problem_text: Roll a fair six-sided die repeatedly.

a.problem_text:
What is the expected number of rolls until you see
two consecutive $5$'s?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Use states based on the last roll.}
We only need to know whether the most recent roll was a $5$.

Define states:
\[
0:\ \text{the last roll is not }5\ \text{(or we are at the start)},\qquad
1:\ \text{the last roll is }5,\qquad
2:\ \text{we have seen }55\ \text{(absorbing)}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Define expected remaining rolls from each state.}
Let
\[
E_0:=\mathbb{E}[\text{additional rolls to reach state 2}\mid \text{state }0],
\]
\[
E_1:=\mathbb{E}[\text{additional rolls to reach state 2}\mid \text{state }1],
\qquad
E_2:=0.
\]
We want $E_0$.

\par\textbf{Step 3: Write first-step recursions.}
From state $0$, roll once:
\begin{itemize}
\item with probability $1/6$ you roll a $5$ and move to state $1$,
\item with probability $5/6$ you roll something else and stay in state $0$.
\end{itemize}
Thus
\[
E_0 = 1 + \frac16 E_1 + \frac56 E_0.
\]

From state $1$, roll once:
\begin{itemize}
\item with probability $1/6$ you roll a $5$ and reach state $2$ (done),
\item with probability $5/6$ you roll something else and go back to state $0$.
\end{itemize}
Thus
\[
E_1 = 1 + \frac16\cdot 0 + \frac56 E_0
= 1 + \frac56 E_0.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Solve the system.}
Rearrange the first equation:
\[
E_0 - \frac56 E_0 = 1 + \frac16 E_1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\frac16 E_0 = 1 + \frac16 E_1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_0 = 6 + E_1.
\]
Substitute $E_1 = 1 + \frac56 E_0$:
\[
E_0 = 6 + 1 + \frac56 E_0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_0 - \frac56 E_0 = 7
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\frac16 E_0 = 7
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_0 = 42.
\]

b.problem_text:
Let $T_n$ be the number of rolls until you first see
$n$ consecutive $5$'s. Find $\mathbb{E}[T_n]$.

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Set up states by the current run length of consecutive 5's.}
Let the state be the number of consecutive $5$'s at the end of the sequence.
States are
\[
0,1,2,\dots,n,
\]
where $n$ is absorbing (we have seen $n$ consecutive $5$'s).

\par\textbf{Step 2: Define the expected remaining time from each state.}
Let
\[
E_k := \mathbb{E}[\text{additional rolls to reach state }n \mid \text{currently in state }k],
\qquad k=0,1,\dots,n.
\]
Then
\[
E_n=0,\qquad \mathbb{E}[T_n]=E_0.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: First-step recursion.}
From any state $k<n$, roll once:
\begin{itemize}
\item with probability $1/6$ you roll a $5$ and move to state $k+1$,
\item with probability $5/6$ you roll something else and reset to state $0$.
\end{itemize}
Thus for $k=0,1,\dots,n-1$,
\[
E_k = 1 + \frac16 E_{k+1} + \frac56 E_0.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Derive a clean recursion for $m_n:=E_0$.}
Use the same ``restart'' trick as in the coin case.
Let $m_n := \mathbb{E}[T_n]$.

Once you first reach $(n-1)$ consecutive $5$'s, to get $n$ consecutive $5$'s you need:
\begin{itemize}
\item one more roll; with probability $1/6$ it is a $5$ and you finish,
\item with probability $5/6$ it is not a $5$, you have used $1$ roll and you restart the whole problem (expected $m_n$ more rolls).
\end{itemize}
Hence the expected additional rolls from the moment you first have $(n-1)$ consecutive $5$'s is
\[
\mathbb{E}[R_n] = \frac16\cdot 1 + \frac56\cdot (1+m_n)
= 1 + \frac56 m_n.
\]
Therefore
\[
m_n = m_{n-1} + \mathbb{E}[R_n]
= m_{n-1} + 1 + \frac56 m_n.
\]
Rearrange:
\[
m_n\left(1-\frac56\right) = m_{n-1}+1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\frac16 m_n = m_{n-1}+1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\boxed{m_n = 6m_{n-1} + 6.}
\]

\par\textbf{Step 5: Base case.}
For $n=1$, $T_1$ is geometric with success probability $1/6$, so
\[
m_1=\mathbb{E}[T_1]=6.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 6: Solve the recursion.}
Solve
\[
m_n = 6m_{n-1}+6,\qquad m_1=6.
\]
Let $u_n := m_n + \frac{6}{5}$. Then
\[
u_n = 6m_{n-1}+6+\frac{6}{5}
= 6\left(m_{n-1}+\frac{6}{5}\right)
= 6u_{n-1}.
\]
Also
\[
u_1 = m_1 + \frac{6}{5} = 6+\frac{6}{5}=\frac{36}{5}.
\]
Thus
\[
u_n = u_1\cdot 6^{n-1} = \frac{36}{5}\,6^{n-1}.
\]
Therefore
\[
m_n = u_n - \frac{6}{5}
= \frac{36}{5}\,6^{n-1} - \frac{6}{5}
= \frac{6}{5}\left(6^n - 1\right).
\]

problems.keyword: runs of successes; consecutive outcomes; waiting time; geometric distribution; expected stopping time; Markov chain; state reset; first-step analysis; recursive expectation; recurrence relation; renewal process; absorbing states; Bernoulli trials; pattern occurrence; dice probability; stochastic process; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Expected Waiting Time for the Pattern $THH$
problems.problem_text: Toss a fair coin repeatedly. What is the expected number of tosses until the pattern $THH$ first appears?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Use states = longest suffix that is a prefix of $THH$.}
The target pattern is $THH$. Its proper prefixes are
\[
\emptyset,\quad T,\quad TH,
\]
and the absorbing state is $THH$.

Let the state describe the longest suffix of the observed sequence that matches a prefix of $THH$:
\[
0:\emptyset,\qquad 1:T,\qquad 2:TH,\qquad 3:THH\ (\text{absorbing}).
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Define expected remaining tosses from each state.}
Let
\[
E_i := \mathbb{E}[\text{additional tosses to reach state 3}\mid \text{currently in state }i],
\quad i=0,1,2,3.
\]
We want $E_0$, and
\[
E_3=0.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Write the transition rules.}
From each state, append the next toss and keep the longest suffix that is a prefix of $THH$.

\begin{itemize}
\item From $0$ ($\emptyset$): $T\to 1$, $H\to 0$.
\item From $1$ ($T$): $H\to 2$ (now have $TH$), $T\to 1$ (still have suffix $T$).
\item From $2$ ($TH$): $H\to 3$ (hit $THH$), $T\to 1$ (suffix becomes $T$).
\end{itemize}

\par\textbf{Step 4: First-step recursions (fair coin).}
Each step uses 1 toss plus the expected remaining tosses from the next state:

\[
E_0 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_1 + \tfrac12 E_0,
\]
\[
E_1 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_2 + \tfrac12 E_1,
\]
\[
E_2 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_3 + \tfrac12 E_1
= 1 + 0 + \tfrac12 E_1.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 5: Solve the system.}

From $E_0$:
\[
E_0 - \tfrac12 E_0 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_0 = 2 + E_1.
\]

From $E_1$:
\[
E_1 - \tfrac12 E_1 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_2
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_1 = 2 + E_2.
\]

Use the equation for $E_2$:
\[
E_2 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_1.
\]

Substitute into $E_1=2+E_2$:
\[
E_1 = 2 + \left(1 + \tfrac12 E_1\right)
= 3 + \tfrac12 E_1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\tfrac12 E_1 = 3
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_1 = 6.
\]
Then
\[
E_0 = 2 + E_1 = 8.
\]

problems.keyword: pattern waiting time; coin tossing; sequence detection; stopping time; expected hitting time; Markov chain; state machine; prefix–suffix states; first-step analysis; recursive expectation; absorbing states; renewal process; runs and patterns; Bernoulli trials; stochastic process; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Expected Payoff with Conditional Rerolls
problems.problem_text:
Roll a fair die. Each roll pays its face value. If you roll $4,5,6$ you may roll again;
if you roll $1,2,3$ the game stops. Find the expected total payoff.

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Let $V$ be the expected total payoff from the start.}
After the first roll, you always receive that face value, and then you either stop or continue.

\par\textbf{Step 2: Condition on the first roll (first-step analysis).}
For outcomes $1,2,3$, the game ends immediately with payoff equal to the roll.
For outcomes $4,5,6$, you receive the roll and then the expected remaining payoff is again $V$
(because the game restarts in the same situation).

Thus
\[
V
=
\frac{1}{6}(1+2+3)
+
\frac{1}{6}\big[(4+V)+(5+V)+(6+V)\big].
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Simplify.}
Compute the sums:
\[
1+2+3=6,\qquad 4+5+6=15.
\]
So
\[
V = \frac{6}{6} + \frac{1}{6}\big(15 + 3V\big)
= 1 + \frac{15}{6} + \frac{3V}{6}.
\]
Hence
\[
V = 1 + \frac{5}{2} + \frac{1}{2}V
= \frac{7}{2} + \frac{1}{2}V.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Solve for $V$.}
\[
V - \frac{1}{2}V = \frac{7}{2}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\frac{1}{2}V = \frac{7}{2}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
V = 7.
\]

problems.keyword: stopping time; Wald’s identity; geometric distribution; expected total reward; renewal process; first-step analysis; recursive expectation; Markov decision process; i.i.d. sequence; random sum; optimal stopping intuition; dice probability; absorbing condition; expected value; stochastic process; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Expected Waiting Time for the First Ace
problems.problem_text:
Draw cards uniformly at random \emph{without replacement} from a standard $52$-card deck.
What is the expected number of draws until the first ace appears?
problems.hint: The 4 aces split the deck into $5$ ``gaps'' on average of equal size, so the first ace tends to show up about one-fifth of the way into the deck.

hints.hint_text: The 4 aces split the deck into $5$ ``gaps'' on average of equal size, so the first ace tends to show up about one-fifth of the way into the deck.

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Model the ace positions.}
Shuffle the deck uniformly at random. Let the positions of the four aces be
\[
1\le A_{(1)} < A_{(2)} < A_{(3)} < A_{(4)} \le 52,
\]
where $A_{(1)}$ is the position of the \emph{first} ace in the shuffled order.
Then the number of draws until the first ace is exactly $A_{(1)}$.
So we want $\mathbb{E}[A_{(1)}]$.

\par\textbf{Step 2: Use the symmetry fact for expected order statistics of discrete uniforms.}
Choose 4 distinct positions uniformly from $\{1,2,\dots,52\}$; these are the ace positions.
A standard symmetry result (discrete analog of the continuous case) is:
\[
\mathbb{E}[A_{(k)}] = \frac{k(52+1)}{4+1},\qquad k=1,2,3,4.
\]
For $k=1$,
\[
\mathbb{E}[A_{(1)}] = \frac{1\cdot 53}{5}=\frac{53}{5}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Interpret the value.}
Thus
\[
\boxed{\mathbb{E}[\text{$\#$ draws until first ace}] = \frac{53}{5}=10.6.}
\]

problems.keyword: negative hypergeometric distribution; sampling without replacement; order statistics; expected minimum; random permutation; first occurrence; symmetry argument; uniform positions; spacing argument; occupancy gaps; expected waiting time; card probability; combinatorial probability; stochastic ordering; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Expected Time to Observe All Six Faces
problems.problem_text: Roll a fair six-sided die repeatedly. What is the expected number of rolls needed to see all six faces at least once?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Decompose the waiting time into stages (coupon collector).}
Let $T$ be the total number of rolls to see all six faces.
Define $T_k$ = the number of additional rolls needed to go from having seen $k$ distinct faces
to having seen $k+1$ distinct faces, for $k=0,1,2,3,4,5$.

Then
\[
T = T_0+T_1+T_2+T_3+T_4+T_5,
\qquad
\mathbb{E}[T] = \sum_{k=0}^5 \mathbb{E}[T_k].
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Compute $\mathbb{E}[T_k]$ using a geometric waiting time.}
If you have already seen $k$ distinct faces, then there are $6-k$ new faces remaining.
On the next roll, the probability of getting a \emph{new} face is
\[
p_k = \frac{6-k}{6}.
\]
So $T_k$ is geometric with success probability $p_k$, hence
\[
\mathbb{E}[T_k] = \frac{1}{p_k} = \frac{6}{6-k}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Sum the expectations.}
Therefore
\[
\mathbb{E}[T]
=
\sum_{k=0}^5 \frac{6}{6-k}
=
6\left(1+\frac12+\frac13+\frac14+\frac15+\frac16\right).
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Simplify to a closed form.}
Compute the harmonic sum:
\[
1+\frac12+\frac13+\frac14+\frac15+\frac16
=
\frac{49}{20}.
\]
Thus
\[
\mathbb{E}[T] = 6\cdot \frac{49}{20}=\frac{294}{20}=\frac{147}{10}=14.7.
\]

problems.keyword: coupon collector problem; expected waiting time; geometric distribution; harmonic numbers; distinct outcomes; sampling with replacement; occupancy problem; stage decomposition; linearity of expectation; stochastic process; discrete probability; expected stopping time; dice probability; classical probability problem; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: The Classical Coupon Collector Problem
problems.problem_text:
There are $N$ distinct coupon types. Each cereal box contains one coupon, and each type is
equally likely (probability $1/N$), independently across boxes.

a.problem_text: How many boxes are needed on average to collect at least one of each type?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Define the total time and break it into stages.}
Let $T$ be the number of boxes needed to obtain all $N$ types.

After you have collected $k$ distinct types, define $T_k$ to be the number of \emph{additional}
boxes needed to obtain a \emph{new} type (so you go from $k$ types to $k+1$ types),
for $k=0,1,\dots,N-1$.

Then
\[
T = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} T_k,
\qquad
\mathbb{E}[T] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\mathbb{E}[T_k].
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Identify the distribution of each stage waiting time.}
When you already have $k$ distinct types, there are $N-k$ missing types.
The probability that the next box contains a \emph{new} type is
\[
p_k = \frac{N-k}{N}.
\]
Hence $T_k$ is geometric with success probability $p_k$, so
\[
\mathbb{E}[T_k] = \frac{1}{p_k} = \frac{N}{N-k}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Sum the expectations.}
Therefore
\[
\mathbb{E}[T]
=
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\frac{N}{N-k}
=
N\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\frac{1}{N-k}
=
N\sum_{j=1}^{N}\frac{1}{j}.
\]
The sum
\[
H_N := \sum_{j=1}^{N}\frac{1}{j}
\]
is the $N$th harmonic number. Thus
\[
\boxed{\mathbb{E}[T]=N H_N.}
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Asymptotic approximation (optional).}
Using the standard approximation
\[
H_N = \ln N + \gamma + o(1),
\]
where $\gamma$ is the Euler--Mascheroni constant, we get
\[
\mathbb{E}[T] \approx N(\ln N + \gamma)
\quad \text{for large }N.
\]

b.problem_text:
After opening $n$ boxes, what is the expected number of \emph{distinct}
coupon types collected?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Define indicator variables for whether each type appears.}
For each type $i\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, define
\[
I_i :=
\mathbf{1}\{\text{type $i$ appears at least once among the $n$ coupons}\}.
\]
Let $D_n$ be the number of distinct types collected after $n$ boxes. Then
\[
D_n=\sum_{i=1}^{N} I_i.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Take expectation using linearity.}
By linearity of expectation,
\[
\mathbb{E}[D_n] = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{E}[I_i]
= \sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{P}(I_i=1).
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Compute $\mathbb{P}(I_i=1)$.}
A fixed type $i$ is \emph{missing} from the first $n$ coupons iff each draw is not $i$.
Since each draw equals $i$ with probability $1/N$,
\[
\mathbb{P}(I_i=0) = \left(1-\frac{1}{N}\right)^n.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mathbb{P}(I_i=1)=1-\left(1-\frac{1}{N}\right)^n.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Sum over all $N$ types.}
All types are symmetric, so each term is the same:
\[
\mathbb{E}[D_n]
=
\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(1-\left(1-\frac{1}{N}\right)^n\right)
=
N\left(1-\left(1-\frac{1}{N}\right)^n\right).
\]

\par\textbf{Conclusion.}
\[
\boxed{\mathbb{E}[\text{# distinct types after $n$ coupons}]
=
N\left(1-\left(1-\frac{1}{N}\right)^n\right).}
\]

\par\textbf{Optional approximation (large $N$).}
Using $\left(1-\frac{1}{N}\right)^n \approx e^{-n/N}$ for large $N$,
\[
\mathbb{E}[D_n]\approx N\left(1-e^{-n/N}\right).
\]

problems.keyword: coupon collector; distinct elements; expected number of uniques; indicator variables; linearity of expectation; occupancy problem; sampling with replacement; Bernoulli indicators; probability of absence; complement probability; symmetry argument; exponential approximation; Poissonization intuition; discrete probability; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Probability Each Player Receives an Ace
problems.problem_text:
A standard $52$-card deck is dealt uniformly at random to $4$ players, $13$ cards each.
What is the probability that \emph{each} player receives at least one ace?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Count the total number of equally likely deals.}
A deal is an assignment of $13$ cards to each labeled player. The total number of deals is
\[
\#\Omega=\binom{52}{13}\binom{39}{13}\binom{26}{13}\binom{13}{13}
=\frac{52!}{(13!)^4}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Count the favorable deals by distributing the aces first.}
There are $4$ aces. For each player to have an ace, the only possibility is:
\[
\text{each player gets exactly 1 ace.}
\]
The number of ways to assign the 4 distinct aces to the 4 labeled players is
\[
4! .
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Deal the remaining $48$ non-ace cards.}
After giving each player $1$ ace, each player still needs $12$ more cards from the $48$ non-aces.
The number of ways to deal these $48$ cards into 4 labeled hands of size $12$ is
\[
\binom{48}{12}\binom{36}{12}\binom{24}{12}\binom{12}{12}
=\frac{48!}{(12!)^4}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Multiply to get the number of favorable deals.}
\[
\#\{\text{favorable}\}=4!\cdot \frac{48!}{(12!)^4}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 5: Divide favorable by total.}
\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{each player has an ace})
=
\frac{4!\cdot \dfrac{48!}{(12!)^4}}{\dfrac{52!}{(13!)^4}}
=
4!\cdot \frac{48!}{52!}\cdot \frac{(13!)^4}{(12!)^4}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 6: Simplify factorial ratios.}
Note that
\[
\frac{48!}{52!}=\frac{1}{52\cdot 51\cdot 50\cdot 49},
\qquad
\frac{13!}{12!}=13.
\]
So
\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{each player has an ace})
=
4!\cdot \frac{13^4}{52\cdot 51\cdot 50\cdot 49}.
\]

Since $4!=24$ and $52=4\cdot 13$, one clean final form is
\[
\boxed{
\mathbb{P}(\text{each player has an ace})
=
\frac{24\cdot 13^4}{52\cdot 51\cdot 50\cdot 49}
=
\frac{24\cdot 13^3}{51\cdot 50\cdot 49}.
}
\]

problems.keyword: card dealing; combinatorial probability; multivariate hypergeometric distribution; random partition; occupancy problem; factorial counting; symmetry argument; distribution without replacement; favorable over total counting; labeled groups; ace distribution; combinatorics; sampling without replacement; classical probability; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Expected Number of Empty Boxes in Random Allocation
problems.problem_text:
Place $18$ balls independently and uniformly at random into $10$ boxes (each ball chooses one box with probability $1/10$).
What is the expected number of empty boxes?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Define indicator variables for emptiness.}
For box $j\in\{1,\dots,10\}$, define
\[
I_j := \mathbf{1}\{\text{box $j$ is empty after all 18 balls are placed}\}.
\]
Let
\[
E := \text{number of empty boxes} = \sum_{j=1}^{10} I_j.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Use linearity of expectation.}
\[
\mathbb{E}[E] = \sum_{j=1}^{10} \mathbb{E}[I_j]
= \sum_{j=1}^{10} \mathbb{P}(I_j=1).
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Compute $\mathbb{P}(I_j=1)$ for a fixed box.}
A given box $j$ is empty iff none of the 18 balls lands in it.
Each ball avoids box $j$ with probability $9/10$, independently, hence
\[
\mathbb{P}(I_j=1) = \left(\frac{9}{10}\right)^{18}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Sum over all boxes.}
All boxes are symmetric, so
\[
\mathbb{E}[E]
=
10\left(\frac{9}{10}\right)^{18}.
\]

problems.keyword: balls and bins; occupancy problem; expected number of empty boxes; indicator variables; linearity of expectation; symmetry argument; independent trials; binomial model; probability of zero count; multinomial distribution; sampling with replacement; discrete probability; random allocation; classical probability; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Winning Probability in an Alternating Pattern Game
problems.problem_text:
Two players $A$ and $B$ alternate flipping a fair coin ($A$ starts). The sequence of outcomes is recorded.
If the pattern $HT$ appears (a head immediately followed by a tail), the game ends and the player who
flips the tail wins. What is $\mathbb{P}(A\text{ wins})$?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Identify the only relevant state information.}
The game ends the first time we see a tail that is \emph{immediately preceded} by a head.
So we only need to know whether the \emph{previous} flip was a head.

Define two transient states:
\[
S_0:\ \text{previous flip is not }H \ (\text{start or previous was }T),
\qquad
S_1:\ \text{previous flip is }H.
\]
If we are in state $S_1$ and the next flip is $T$, the game ends and the player who flipped that $T$ wins.

\par\textbf{Step 2: Define win probabilities depending on whose turn it is.}
Let
\[
a_0=\mathbb{P}(A\text{ wins}\mid \text{A to flip next and state }S_0),\qquad
a_1=\mathbb{P}(A\text{ wins}\mid \text{A to flip next and state }S_1),
\]
\[
b_0=\mathbb{P}(A\text{ wins}\mid \text{B to flip next and state }S_0),\qquad
b_1=\mathbb{P}(A\text{ wins}\mid \text{B to flip next and state }S_1).
\]
We want $a_0$ (start: A flips first, and there is no previous head).

\par\textbf{Step 3: Write first-step recursions (fair coin).}

\par\underline{A's turn, state $S_0$.}
If A flips $H$ (prob. $1/2$) $\Rightarrow$ state becomes $S_1$ and it is B's turn.
If A flips $T$ (prob. $1/2$) $\Rightarrow$ state remains $S_0$ and it is B's turn.
So
\[
a_0=\tfrac12 b_1+\tfrac12 b_0.
\]

\par\underline{A's turn, state $S_1$.}
If A flips $T$ (prob. $1/2$), the pattern $HT$ occurs and A flipped the tail, so A wins immediately.
If A flips $H$ (prob. $1/2$), the state stays $S_1$ and it is B's turn.
Thus
\[
a_1=\tfrac12\cdot 1+\tfrac12 b_1.
\]

\par\underline{B's turn, state $S_0$.}
If B flips $H$ (prob. $1/2$) $\Rightarrow$ state $S_1$, A's turn.
If B flips $T$ (prob. $1/2$) $\Rightarrow$ state $S_0$, A's turn.
Thus
\[
b_0=\tfrac12 a_1+\tfrac12 a_0.
\]

\par\underline{B's turn, state $S_1$.}
If B flips $T$ (prob. $1/2$), then $HT$ occurs and B flipped the tail, so A loses (prob.\ $0$ of A winning).
If B flips $H$ (prob. $1/2$), state stays $S_1$ and it is A's turn.
Hence
\[
b_1=\tfrac12\cdot 0+\tfrac12 a_1=\tfrac12 a_1.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Solve the system.}
From $b_1=\tfrac12 a_1$, plug into $a_1$:
\[
a_1=\tfrac12+\tfrac12 b_1=\tfrac12+\tfrac12\left(\tfrac12 a_1\right)
=\tfrac12+\tfrac14 a_1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
a_1-\tfrac14 a_1=\tfrac12
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\tfrac34 a_1=\tfrac12
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
a_1=\frac{2}{3}.
\]
Then
\[
b_1=\tfrac12 a_1=\frac{1}{3}.
\]

Now use the pair
\[
a_0=\tfrac12(b_1+b_0),\qquad b_0=\tfrac12(a_1+a_0).
\]
Substitute $b_0$ into $a_0$:
\[
a_0=\tfrac12 b_1+\tfrac12\cdot \tfrac12(a_1+a_0)
=\tfrac12 b_1+\tfrac14 a_1+\tfrac14 a_0.
\]
So
\[
a_0-\tfrac14 a_0=\tfrac12 b_1+\tfrac14 a_1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\tfrac34 a_0=\tfrac12\cdot \frac13+\tfrac14\cdot \frac23
=\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{3}.
\]
Therefore
\[
a_0=\frac{1/3}{3/4}=\frac{4}{9}.
\]

problems.keyword: sequential coin game; alternating turns; pattern stopping time; first occurrence of pattern; Markov chain states; state transition analysis; first-step recursion; conditional probability; absorbing states; stochastic game; pattern matching (HT); recursive equations; symmetry and turn advantage; hitting probability; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Extra Coin Advantage
problems.problem_text:
Player $A$ flips $n+1$ fair coins and player $B$ flips $n$ fair coins.
Let $H_A$ and $H_B$ be their numbers of heads. Find
\[
\mathbb{P}(H_A > H_B).
\]

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Split $A$'s coins into $n$ coins plus one extra coin.}
Write
\[
H_A = H_A' + Z,
\]
where
\[
H_A' \sim \mathrm{Bin}(n,\tfrac12),\qquad Z\sim \mathrm{Bern}(\tfrac12),
\]
and $H_A'$ is independent of $Z$.
Also
\[
H_B \sim \mathrm{Bin}(n,\tfrac12),
\]
independent of $(H_A',Z)$.

\par\textbf{Step 2: Use symmetry for two i.i.d. binomials.}
Since $H_A'$ and $H_B$ are i.i.d.,
\[
\mathbb{P}(H_A' > H_B) = \mathbb{P}(H_B > H_A'),
\]
so
\[
\mathbb{P}(H_A' > H_B) = \frac{1-\mathbb{P}(H_A'=H_B)}{2}.
\]
Let
\[
\pi := \mathbb{P}(H_A'=H_B).
\]
Then
\[
\mathbb{P}(H_A' > H_B)=\frac{1-\pi}{2}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Express $\mathbb{P}(H_A > H_B)$ using the extra coin.}
Condition on $Z$:

\par If $Z=1$ (prob. $1/2$), then
\[
H_A > H_B \iff H_A'+1 > H_B \iff H_A' \ge H_B.
\]

\par If $Z=0$ (prob. $1/2$), then
\[
H_A > H_B \iff H_A' > H_B.
\]

Therefore
\[
\mathbb{P}(H_A>H_B)
=
\frac12\,\mathbb{P}(H_A'\ge H_B)
+
\frac12\,\mathbb{P}(H_A'>H_B).
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Rewrite $\mathbb{P}(H_A'\ge H_B)$ and simplify.}
We have
\[
\mathbb{P}(H_A'\ge H_B)=1-\mathbb{P}(H_A' < H_B)
=1-\mathbb{P}(H_B > H_A').
\]
But $\mathbb{P}(H_B > H_A')=\mathbb{P}(H_A' > H_B)=\frac{1-\pi}{2}$ by Step 2. Hence
\[
\mathbb{P}(H_A'\ge H_B)=1-\frac{1-\pi}{2}=\frac{1+\pi}{2}.
\]

Now plug into Step 3:
\[
\mathbb{P}(H_A>H_B)
=
\frac12\cdot \frac{1+\pi}{2}
+
\frac12\cdot \frac{1-\pi}{2}
=
\frac{1}{4}\big((1+\pi)+(1-\pi)\big)
=
\frac12.
\]

\par\textbf{Conclusion.}
\[
\boxed{\mathbb{P}(H_A>H_B)=\frac12.}
\]

problems.keyword: binomial distribution; coin flipping; comparison of sums; symmetry argument; exchangeability; tie probability; conditioning on extra trial; decomposition method; Bernoulli variable; identical distributions; probabilistic symmetry; cancellation effect; discrete probability; fair coins; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: The Ticket Booth Change Problem
problems.problem_text:
There are $2n$ people in a fixed line. Exactly $n$ have only \$5 bills and $n$ have only \$10 bills.
Each ticket costs \$5. The seller starts with no change.
What is the probability that everyone can buy a ticket in this fixed order (i.e. no one has to swap positions)?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Translate the condition into a prefix constraint.}

Each \$5 customer increases the cashier's number of \$5 bills by $1$.

Each \$10 customer requires one \$5 bill as change, decreasing the count by $1$.

Let
\[
S_k = (\#\text{\$5 customers among first $k$}) - (\#\text{\$10 customers among first $k$}).
\]
The process works if and only if
\[
S_k \ge 0 \quad \text{for all } k=1,2,\dots,2n.
\]
Since there are exactly $n$ customers of each type,
\[
S_{2n}=0.
\]

\par
\textbf{Step 2: Encode as a lattice path.}

Represent:
\[
\$5 \rightarrow +1 \quad (\text{up step}), \qquad
\$10 \rightarrow -1 \quad (\text{down step}).
\]

Then each possible line corresponds to a path of $2n$ steps with $n$ up-steps and $n$ down-steps.

Total number of possible lines:
\[
\binom{2n}{n}.
\]

We must count how many such paths never go below $0$.

\par
\textbf{Step 3: Use the reflection principle.}

Call a path:
\begin{itemize}
\item \emph{good} if $S_k \ge 0$ for all $k$,
\item \emph{bad} otherwise.
\end{itemize}

A bad path must hit level $-1$ at some time.
Let $k$ be the \emph{first} time such that
\[
S_k = -1.
\]

\par
\textbf{Step 4: Reflection mapping.}

For a bad path, reflect the first $k$ steps by swapping
\[
+1 \leftrightarrow -1.
\]
That is:
\begin{itemize}
\item up-steps become down-steps,
\item down-steps become up-steps,
\item steps after time $k$ remain unchanged.
\end{itemize}

\par
\textbf{Step 5: Effect of reflection.}

At time $k$ before reflection:
\[
\#\text{downs} = \#\text{ups} + 1.
\]
After reflection, this becomes
\[
\#\text{ups} = \#\text{downs} + 1.
\]

Since the original full path had $n$ ups and $n$ downs, the reflected path has
\[
n+1 \text{ ups}, \qquad n-1 \text{ downs}.
\]

The reflection mapping is one-to-one and onto sequences with $n+1$ ups and $n-1$ downs.

Thus
\[
\#\{\text{bad paths}\} = \binom{2n}{n+1}.
\]

\par
\textbf{Step 6: Count good paths.}

\[
\#\{\text{good}\}
=
\binom{2n}{n} - \binom{2n}{n+1}.
\]

Using
\[
\binom{2n}{n+1} = \frac{n}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n},
\]
we obtain
\[
\#\{\text{good}\}
=
\left(1 - \frac{n}{n+1}\right)\binom{2n}{n}
=
\frac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n}.
\]

\par
\textbf{Step 7: Compute the probability.}

\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{everyone can buy in order})
=
\frac{\#\text{good}}{\#\text{total}}
=
\frac{\frac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n}}{\binom{2n}{n}}
=
\boxed{\frac{1}{n+1}}.
\]

problems.keyword: reflection principle; Catalan numbers; ballot problem; Bertrand ballot theorem; prefix constraint; lattice paths; Dyck paths; random walk with absorbing boundary; combinatorial probability; path counting; symmetry argument; binomial coefficients; change-making problem; queue feasibility; discrete stochastic processes; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: The Monty Hall Problem
problems.problem_text:
There are 3 doors: behind 1 is a car, behind 2 are goats. You choose a door. Monty (who knows
where the car is) opens one of the other two doors that has a goat. You may stay or switch
to the remaining unopened door. Should you switch, and what is the winning probability if you switch?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Define the key events.}
Let
\[
C=\{\text{your initial choice has the car}\},\qquad C^c=\{\text{your initial choice has a goat}\}.
\]
Initially, before Monty opens any door,
\[
\mathbb{P}(C)=\frac13,\qquad \mathbb{P}(C^c)=\frac23.
\]
\textbf{Step 2: Use the fact that Monty never opens the car door.}
Monty always opens a door you did \emph{not} choose, and he always reveals a goat.
So Monty's action does not change whether your \emph{initial} door was correct:
it simply reveals information about the other doors.

\par\textbf{Step 3: Analyze what happens if you switch.}
There are two cases:

\par\textbf{Case 1: Your initial choice is the car ($C$).}
This happens with probability $1/3$.
Monty opens a goat door among the other two.
If you \emph{switch}, you must switch away from the car, so you lose.
\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{win by switching}\mid C)=0.
\]

\par\textbf{Case 2: Your initial choice is a goat ($C^c$).}
This happens with probability $2/3$.
Then the car is behind one of the two doors you did \emph{not} choose.
Monty opens the \emph{other} goat door (he is forced to avoid the car),
so the remaining unopened door must be the car.
If you \emph{switch}, you win.
\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{win by switching}\mid C^c)=1.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Combine by total probability.}
\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{win by switching})
=
\mathbb{P}(C)\cdot 0 + \mathbb{P}(C^c)\cdot 1
=
\frac13\cdot 0 + \frac23\cdot 1
=
\frac23.
\]
\textbf{Conclusion.}
\[
\boxed{\text{You should switch. If you switch, your probability of winning is } \frac23. \text{Otherwise, it is} \frac13.}
\]

problems.keyword: Monty Hall problem; conditional probability; Bayesian updating; information revelation; host strategy; dependent events; law of total probability; decision strategy; switching advantage; posterior probability; hidden information; counterintuitive probability; game show paradox; optimal strategy; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Probability Your Card Beats the Dealer's
problems.problem_text:
A standard deck has $13$ ranks (values), each appearing in $4$ suits.
You draw one card, then the dealer draws one card \emph{without replacement}.
Assume ranks are compared only by value (ties allowed). What is the probability that
your rank is larger than the dealer's rank?
problems.hint: Let $Y$ be your rank and $D$ be the dealer's rank. Then \[ \mathbb{P}(Y > D)+\mathbb{P}(Y < D)+\mathbb{P}(Y = D)=1. \]

hints.hint_text: Let $Y$ be your rank and $D$ be the dealer's rank. Then \[ \mathbb{P}(Y > D)+\mathbb{P}(Y < D)+\mathbb{P}(Y = D)=1. \]
By symmetry, \[ \mathbb{P}(Y > D)=\mathbb{P}(D > Y). \]

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Use symmetry between the two players.}
Let $Y$ be your rank and $D$ be the dealer's rank.

By symmetry of the dealing procedure (one card to you, one to dealer),
\[
\mathbb{P}(Y > D)=\mathbb{P}(D > Y).
\]
Also, exactly one of the three disjoint events happens:
\[
\{Y > D\},\quad \{Y = D\},\quad \{Y < D\}.
\]
Thus
\[
\mathbb{P}(Y > D)+\mathbb{P}(Y < D)+\mathbb{P}(Y = D)=1,
\]
and with $\mathbb{P}(Y > D)=\mathbb{P}(Y < D)$ we get
\[
2\mathbb{P}(Y > D)=1-\mathbb{P}(Y = D)
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathbb{P}(Y > D)=\frac{1-\mathbb{P}(Y = D)}{2}.
\]

So it remains to compute $\mathbb{P}(Y = D)$.

\par\textbf{Step 2: Compute the tie probability $\mathbb{P}(Y = D)$.}
Condition on your first card's rank.
Whatever rank you draw, there are $3$ remaining cards of the same rank among the remaining $51$ cards.
Hence
\[
\mathbb{P}(Y=D)=\frac{3}{51}=\frac{1}{17}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Plug into the symmetry formula.}
\[
\mathbb{P}(Y>D)=\frac{1-\frac{1}{17}}{2}
=\frac{\frac{16}{17}}{2}
=\frac{8}{17}.
\]

\par\textbf{Conclusion.}
\[
\boxed{\mathbb{P}(\text{your rank is larger than dealer's})=\frac{8}{17}.}
\]

problems.keyword: card ranks comparison; symmetry argument; tie probability; conditional probability; sampling without replacement; hypergeometric intuition; exchangeability; complementary events; probability decomposition; discrete uniform ranks; deck combinatorics; fairness symmetry; two-player comparison; classical card probability; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Probability All Random Points Lie in a Semicircle
problems.problem_text:
Pick $N$ points independently and uniformly at random on the circumference of a circle.
What is the probability that all $N$ points lie within \emph{some} semicircle?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Fix a reference point using rotational symmetry.}
Because the joint distribution is rotation-invariant, we may condition on (or equivalently,
rotate so that) one of the points is at angle $0$.

A standard way to avoid double counting is this:

\par\textbf{Step 2: Use the ``leftmost point'' (smallest angle) trick.}
Let the points have angles (mod $2\pi$):
\[
\Theta_1,\dots,\Theta_N \quad \text{i.i.d. uniform on }[0,2\pi).
\]
With probability $1$ (continuity), there is a unique point with the smallest angle.
Call that point $\Theta_{(1)}$ (the minimum).

Now consider the semicircle (arc of length $\pi$) that starts at $\Theta_{(1)}$ and goes
counterclockwise:
\[
[\Theta_{(1)},\ \Theta_{(1)}+\pi]\ (\text{mod }2\pi).
\]

\par\textbf{Key claim.}
All $N$ points lie within \emph{some} semicircle
\[
\Longleftrightarrow
\text{all other }N-1\text{ points lie in }[\Theta_{(1)},\Theta_{(1)}+\pi].
\]
\emph{Reason:}
If all points fit in some semicircle, slide/rotate that semicircle clockwise until its left endpoint
hits the first (minimum-angle) point; it still contains all points, and now it is exactly the semicircle
starting at $\Theta_{(1)}$.

\par\textbf{Step 3: Compute the probability conditional on the minimum point.}
Condition on the value of $\Theta_{(1)}$.
Given $\Theta_{(1)}$, the remaining $N-1$ points are still i.i.d. uniform on the circle,
and each has probability
\[
\frac{\text{length of semicircle}}{\text{length of circle}}=\frac{\pi}{2\pi}=\frac12
\]
to fall into the semicircle $[\Theta_{(1)},\Theta_{(1)}+\pi]$.

By independence,
\[
\mathbb{P}\Big(\text{all other }N-1\text{ points lie in that semicircle}\ \Big|\ \Theta_{(1)}\Big)
=
\left(\frac12\right)^{N-1}.
\]
This does not depend on $\Theta_{(1)}$, so unconditioning gives
\[
\mathbb{P}\Big(\text{all points lie within a semicircle that starts at the minimum point}\Big)
=
\left(\frac12\right)^{N-1}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Account for which point is the minimum.}
Any one of the $N$ labeled points is equally likely to be the minimum-angle point, and the events
\[
E_i := \{\text{point $i$ is the minimum and all points lie in the semicircle starting at point $i$}\}
\]
are disjoint across $i$ (there is only one minimum).

Thus
\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{all points lie in some semicircle})
=
\sum_{i=1}^N \mathbb{P}(E_i)
=
N \cdot \mathbb{P}(E_1).
\]
But
\[
\mathbb{P}(E_1) = \mathbb{P}(\text{point 1 is minimum})\cdot \left(\frac12\right)^{N-1}
= \frac{1}{N}\left(\frac12\right)^{N-1}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{all points lie in some semicircle})
=
N\cdot \frac{1}{N}\left(\frac12\right)^{N-1}
=
\frac{N}{2^{\,N-1}}.
\]

\par\textbf{Conclusion.}
\[
\boxed{\mathbb{P}(\text{all $N$ points lie within some semicircle})=\frac{N}{2^{N-1}}.}
\]

problems.keyword: random points on circle; semicircle coverage; geometric probability; rotational symmetry; order statistics on circle; minimum angle method; arc containment; uniform distribution on circumference; symmetry argument; independence; extreme point trick; circular statistics; combinatorial geometry; coverage probability; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: The Derangement Problem
problems.problem_text:
There are $5$ firms and $5$ cover letters, one intended for each firm.
Letters are placed into envelopes uniformly at random (i.e. a random permutation).
What is the probability that \emph{no} letter goes to its correct firm?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Model as a random permutation.}
Label the firms $1,2,3,4,5$. A random assignment of letters to firms is a uniformly random
permutation $\pi$ of $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$, where $\pi(i)$ is the letter sent to firm $i$.

A letter goes to the correct firm exactly when $\pi(i)=i$.
We want
\[
\mathbb{P}(\pi(i)\neq i\ \text{for all }i),
\]
i.e. the probability that $\pi$ has no fixed points.

\par\textbf{Step 2: Count total assignments.}
Total permutations:
\[
5! = 120.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Count derangements using inclusion--exclusion.}
Let $A_i=\{\pi(i)=i\}$ be the event that firm $i$ gets the correct letter.

The number of permutations with \emph{no} fixed points is
\[
!5 = 5! - \binom51 4! + \binom52 3! - \binom53 2! + \binom54 1! - \binom55 0!.
\]
Compute term by term:
\[
5!=120,\quad
\binom51 4!=5\cdot 24=120,\quad
\binom52 3!=10\cdot 6=60,
\]
\[
\binom53 2!=10\cdot 2=20,\quad
\binom54 1!=5\cdot 1=5,\quad
\binom55 0!=1.
\]
So
\[
!5 = 120-120+60-20+5-1 = 44.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Convert to probability.}
\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{all 5 mailed to wrong firms})
=
\frac{!5}{5!}
=
\frac{44}{120}
=
\frac{11}{30}.
\]
\par\textbf{Conclusion.}
\[
\boxed{\mathbb{P}(\text{no letter is correctly mailed})=\frac{11}{30}.}
\]

problems.keyword: derangement problem; permutations without fixed points; matching problem; random permutation; inclusion–exclusion principle; fixed points; hat-check problem; combinatorial probability; factorial counting; rencontres numbers; exact counting; symmetry argument; discrete probability; classical combinatorics; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: The Birthday Paradox
problems.problem_text:
Assume $365$ equally likely birthdays (ignore leap years) and independence.
Find the smallest $n$ such that
\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{at least two people share a birthday})>\frac12.
\]

hints.hint_text: Consider the complement event.

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Use the complement event (all birthdays distinct).}
Let
\[
A_n=\{\text{all $n$ birthdays are distinct}\}.
\]
Then
\[
\mathbb{P}(\text{at least one match}) = 1-\mathbb{P}(A_n).
\]
So we want
\[
1-\mathbb{P}(A_n)>\frac12
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathbb{P}(A_n)<\frac12.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Compute $\mathbb{P}(A_n)$.}
For the first person, any birthday is fine: probability $1$.
For the second person to avoid matching: probability $\frac{364}{365}$.
For the third: probability $\frac{363}{365}$, etc.

Thus for $n\le 365$,
\[
\mathbb{P}(A_n)
=
\frac{365}{365}\cdot\frac{364}{365}\cdot\frac{363}{365}\cdots\frac{365-(n-1)}{365}
=
\prod_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{365-k}{365}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Use a standard exponential bound/approximation.}
Take logs:
\[
\ln \mathbb{P}(A_n) = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\ln\left(1-\frac{k}{365}\right).
\]
Using $\ln(1-x)\le -x$ for $0<x<1$,
\[
\ln \mathbb{P}(A_n)
\le
-\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{k}{365}
=
-\frac{1}{365}\cdot \frac{(n-1)n}{2}.
\]
Exponentiating,
\[
\mathbb{P}(A_n)\le \exp\!\left(-\frac{n(n-1)}{2\cdot 365}\right).
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Solve for when the match probability exceeds $1/2$.}
We want $\mathbb{P}(A_n)<1/2$. A sufficient condition is
\[
\exp\!\left(-\frac{n(n-1)}{2\cdot 365}\right)<\frac12.
\]
Take logs:
\[
-\frac{n(n-1)}{2\cdot 365}<\ln\left(\frac12\right)=-\ln 2
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\frac{n(n-1)}{2\cdot 365}>\ln 2.
\]
So
\[
n(n-1) > 2\cdot 365 \ln 2 \approx 730 \cdot 0.6931 \approx 505.9.
\]
Check nearby integers:
\[
22\cdot 21 = 462 < 505.9,\qquad
23\cdot 22 = 506 > 505.9.
\]
So the threshold should be around $n=23$.

\par\textbf{Step 5: State the exact known smallest $n$.}
Indeed, the exact product satisfies
\[
\mathbb{P}(A_{22})\approx 0.524,\qquad
\mathbb{P}(A_{23})\approx 0.493,
\]
so
\[
1-\mathbb{P}(A_{22})\approx 0.476<\frac12,\qquad
1-\mathbb{P}(A_{23})\approx 0.507>\frac12.
\]
\par\textbf{Conclusion.}
\[
\boxed{\text{The smallest class size is } n=23.}
\]

problems.problem_name: Uniform Sum Stopping-Time Problem
problems.problem_text:
Let $X_1,X_2,\dots$ be i.i.d. $U(0,1)$. Define partial sums $S_n=X_1+\cdots+X_n$ and the stopping time
\[
N:=\min\{n\ge 1:\ S_n>1\}.
\]
Find $\mathbb{E}[N]$.
problems.hint: Recall that for a positive integer-valued random variable, \[ \mathbb{E}[N]=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\mathbb{P}(N>n). \]

hints.hint_text: Recall that for a positive integer-valued random variable, \[ \mathbb{E}[N]=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\mathbb{P}(N>n). \]

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Use the tail-sum formula for expectations.}
For a positive integer-valued random variable,
\[
\mathbb{E}[N]=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\mathbb{P}(N>n).
\]
Here, $N>n$ means we have not exceeded $1$ after $n$ samples, i.e.
\[
\{N>n\}=\{S_n\le 1\}.
\]
(With $S_0=0$, we have $\mathbb{P}(N>0)=1$.)

So
\[
\mathbb{E}[N]=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\mathbb{P}(S_n\le 1).
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Compute $\mathbb{P}(S_n\le 1)$ for $n\ge 1$.}
For $n\ge 1$,
\[
\mathbb{P}(S_n\le 1)=\mathbb{P}(X_1+\cdots+X_n\le 1).
\]
Since $(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ is uniform on the unit cube $[0,1]^n$, this probability equals the volume of
\[
\{(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in[0,1]^n:\ x_1+\cdots+x_n\le 1\}.
\]
This region is the standard $n$-simplex, whose volume is
\[
\frac{1}{n!}.
\]
Hence
\[
\mathbb{P}(S_n\le 1)=\frac{1}{n!}\quad (n\ge 1),
\]
and also $\mathbb{P}(S_0\le 1)=1=\frac{1}{0!}$.

\par\textbf{Step 3: Sum the series.}
Therefore
\[
\mathbb{E}[N]=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n!}.
\]
But
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n!}=e.
\]

problems.keyword: stopping time; random sum; uniform distribution; sequential sampling; expected sample size; tail-sum formula; survival probabilities; simplex probability; geometric probability; unit hypercube volume; factorial probability; exponential constant; renewal-type process; continuous i.i.d. sampling; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Probability the Sum of Uniform Variables Is at Most One
problems.problem_text:
Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be i.i.d. $U(0,1)$. Find
\[
\mathbb{P}(S_n \le 1),\qquad S_n:=X_1+\cdots+X_n.
\]

hints.hint_text: What's $\mathbb{P}(X_1+X_2\leq 1)$?
What's $\mathbb{P}(X_1+X_2 + X_3\leq 1)$?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Interpret the probability as a volume.}
The joint density of $(X_1,\dots,X_n)$ on the unit cube $[0,1]^n$ is $1$.
Therefore,
\[
\mathbb{P}(S_n\le 1)
=
\int_{[0,1]^n} \mathbf{1}\{x_1+\cdots+x_n\le 1\}\,dx_1\cdots dx_n,
\]
which is exactly the $n$-dimensional volume of the region
\[
R := \left\{(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in[0,1]^n:\ x_1+\cdots+x_n\le 1\right\}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Recognize the region as a simplex.}
Because all coordinates are nonnegative in $[0,1]^n$, the constraint
$x_1+\cdots+x_n\le 1$ describes the standard $n$-simplex in $\mathbb{R}^n$:
\[
\Delta_n := \{x_i\ge 0,\ \sum_{i=1}^n x_i \le 1\}.
\]
Notice $\Delta_n \subset [0,1]^n$ automatically (since $x_i\le \sum x_i \le 1$), so the cube constraint
does not further cut the region.

Hence
\[
\mathbb{P}(S_n\le 1) = \operatorname{Vol}(\Delta_n).
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Compute the volume of the simplex by iterated integration.}
Compute
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(\Delta_n)
=
\int_{x_1=0}^{1}\int_{x_2=0}^{1-x_1}\cdots
\int_{x_n=0}^{1-x_1-\cdots-x_{n-1}} dx_n\cdots dx_2\,dx_1.
\]

Integrate in $x_n$ first:
\[
\int_{0}^{1-x_1-\cdots-x_{n-1}} dx_n
=
1-x_1-\cdots-x_{n-1}.
\]
So the volume becomes
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(\Delta_n)
=
\int_{x_1=0}^{1}\int_{x_2=0}^{1-x_1}\cdots
\int_{x_{n-1}=0}^{1-x_1-\cdots-x_{n-2}}
\left(1-x_1-\cdots-x_{n-1}\right)
dx_{n-1}\cdots dx_2\,dx_1.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 4: Use induction on $n$ to finish.}
Let $V_n := \operatorname{Vol}(\Delta_n)$.

\par\emph{Base case:} $n=1$.
\[
V_1 = \operatorname{Vol}\{0\le x_1\le 1\} = 1 = \frac{1}{1!}.
\]

\par\emph{Induction step:} Assume $V_{n-1}=\frac{1}{(n-1)!}$.
Condition on $x_n=t$. Then the remaining coordinates satisfy
\[
x_1+\cdots+x_{n-1}\le 1-t,\qquad x_i\ge 0.
\]
This is a scaled $(n-1)$-simplex, so its volume is
\[
\operatorname{Vol}(\Delta_{n-1}(1-t)) = (1-t)^{n-1} V_{n-1}.
\]
Therefore
\[
V_n = \int_{0}^{1} (1-t)^{n-1} V_{n-1}\,dt
= V_{n-1}\int_{0}^{1}(1-t)^{n-1}\,dt
= V_{n-1}\cdot \frac{1}{n}.
\]
Using the induction hypothesis:
\[
V_n = \frac{1}{(n-1)!}\cdot \frac{1}{n}=\frac{1}{n!}.
\]

problems.keyword: uniform distribution; sum of random variables; simplex probability; geometric probability; volume interpretation; unit hypercube; n-simplex; order statistics connection; Irwin–Hall distribution; multiple integrals; change of variables; induction on dimension; symmetry argument; continuous probability; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: Length of a Random Increasing Run
problems.problem_text:
Let $X_1,X_2,\dots$ be i.i.d. $U(0,1)$. You keep drawing as long as the sequence is
(strictly) monotonically increasing, i.e. you stop at the first time $X_{k+1}\le X_k$.
Let
\[
L := \max\{k\ge 1:\ X_1 < X_2 < \cdots < X_k\}
\]
be the length of the increasing run. Find $\mathbb{E}[L]$.
problems.hint: Recall that for any positive integer-valued random variable, \[ \mathbb{E}[L] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\mathbb{P}(L\ge k). \]

hints.hint_text: Recall that for any positive integer-valued random variable, \[ \mathbb{E}[L] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\mathbb{P}(L\ge k). \]

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Express $\mathbb{E}[L]$ using tail probabilities.}
For any positive integer-valued random variable,
\[
\mathbb{E}[L] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\mathbb{P}(L\ge k).
\]

\par\textbf{Step 2: Compute $\mathbb{P}(L\ge k)$.}
The event $\{L\ge k\}$ means the first $k$ draws are strictly increasing:
\[
\{L\ge k\}=\{X_1 < X_2 < \cdots < X_k\}.
\]

Since $X_1,\dots,X_k$ are i.i.d. continuous, with probability $1$ they are all distinct.
All $k!$ relative orderings of $(X_1,\dots,X_k)$ are equally likely, and exactly \emph{one}
of them is increasing. Hence
\[
\mathbb{P}(X_1 < X_2 < \cdots < X_k)=\frac{1}{k!}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\mathbb{P}(L\ge k)=\frac{1}{k!}.
\]

\par\textbf{Step 3: Sum the series.}
Thus
\[
\mathbb{E}[L]
=
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{k!}.
\]
Recall the Taylor series
\[
e=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{k!}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{k!} = e - 1.
\]

problems.keyword: monotone sequence; increasing run; stopping time; expected run length; order statistics; permutation symmetry; probability of ordered sample; tail-sum formula; expectation via survival probabilities; factorial probability; series expansion; exponential constant; Poisson connection intuition; continuous i.i.d. sampling; interview probability problem

problems.problem_name: The Random Noodle Loop Problem
problems.problem_text: There are $100$ noodles in a bowl. At each step, you randomly select two noodle ends from the bowl and tie them together. What's the expectation on $\#$ of loops formed?

solution.text:
\textbf{Step 1: Track only the number of \emph{open chains}.}
At any moment, the tied structure consists of:
\begin{itemize}
